Q1: I tried to keep things fairly structured, honestly. Being 29 and having been through university before, I know myself well enough to set up a weekly schedule where I'd dedicate certain blocks to coursework. I usually aimed for a few hours in the morning before other responsibilities kicked in, maybe three or four times a week. It wasn't always perfect, but I had a system that I tried to stick to.
Q2: I definitely leaned toward regular times each week, though I'll admit deadlines sometimes threw that off. I'd try to front-load my studying early in the week so I wouldn't be scrambling later. But when multiple assignments piled up across my six courses, I'd have to adjust and put in extra time wherever I could find it. The routine was more of a guideline than a strict rule.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new materials had been posted or seeing if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the lecture videos or check my grades. I tried to make it a habit to log in at least a few times a week, even if just to stay in the loop. It became almost automatic after a while.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became pretty automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I'd open it up, scan through any updates, and plan my day from there. The routine developed naturally because I needed to keep track of six courses simultaneously. It wasn't exciting, but it was necessary to stay organized.
Q5: The lecture videos were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations at my own pace. The course outlines and rubrics were also helpful because they told me exactly what was expected. I appreciated anything that gave me clear structure and visual explanations.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time, even though I posted occasionally. The peer interactions never really clicked for me, and I found I learned better on my own anyway. Some of the supplementary readings also got pushed aside when time was tight. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on. I know that sounds cynical, but with six courses, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. If I could see how it would help me pass an exam or complete an assignment, I was all in. Clear relevance to my grades made all the difference.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't really afford. Required work had a deadline and consequences, so it got done first. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got bumped when time ran short. There was a mental weight difference too—required work carried stress, while optional things felt like they were for people with more time than me.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and PDFs so I could study them offline on my tablet. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand, which helped me remember things better. I also used textbooks and some YouTube videos to supplement what was in the course. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing printed notes or working through problems in a notebook, and none of that would show up anywhere online. I'd sometimes spend hours going over diagrams I'd saved, making flashcards, or just thinking through concepts while walking. The LMS only captured when I was actively clicking around, not when I was genuinely processing the material.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once online to get the gist, then download it or the slides and review them multiple times offline. My internet is reliable, but I found it easier to focus without the browser open and all its distractions. So my single view on the LMS didn't reflect the three or four times I actually went through the material.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. The stress built up because I had exams and assignments converging across multiple courses. My attendance dropped during that period because I was prioritizing study time over live sessions. I also hit a wall emotionally a few times—my resilience isn't great, and I'd feel overwhelmed even though I was technically managing.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from life outside school also meant I sometimes had to choose between engaging deeply with one course or superficially with several. The workload across six courses meant I was always making trade-offs.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos or readings with good intentions but find my mind wandering completely. Sometimes I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without taking in a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was that I'd convince myself I'd do a better job if I waited until I felt more focused. But honestly, some of it was just avoidance—certain tasks felt tedious or overwhelming, so I'd push them off until the deadline forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd structure my LMS usage around what was due soonest, prioritizing accordingly. Without a deadline, I'd often let things slide indefinitely. It's not ideal, but the external pressure of due dates kept me moving forward when motivation alone wasn't enough.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it in time to adjust. If I received comments on an assignment, I'd try to apply them to the next one. But sometimes feedback came too late, or I was already onto the next thing. The CBT sessions I did helped me process feedback without spiraling into self-criticism, which made a difference in how I approached subsequent work.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I appreciated. When modules were clearly laid out with dates and expectations, I could plan my weeks effectively. But when things were vague or scattered, I'd spend more time figuring out what to do than actually doing it. Clear structure reduced my stress significantly.
Q19: Having everything in one place on the LMS made engagement easier—I didn't have to hunt for materials. But the sheer volume of content across six courses sometimes made it overwhelming to even open the platform. Clearer prioritization of essential versus supplementary materials would have helped me focus on what truly mattered.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're watching videos, reading materials, and genuinely trying, but can't translate that into completed assignments. It could also be perfectionism or anxiety making it hard to submit something that feels unfinished.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I didn't have the highest LMS activity, but I passed because a lot of my real learning happened offline. Someone might download everything early, study from printed materials, and only show up online for submissions. The LMS doesn't capture studying from textbooks or handwritten notes.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you spent. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if I was deeply focused or completely zoned out. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the discussions with friends, and the genuine understanding that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement is mental investment—feeling like the course matters and that I'm genuinely learning something, not just surviving until the grade comes in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with stress and low resilience throughout the semester. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety around deadlines. It also wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes, the mental effort of balancing six courses, or the genuine motivation I had even when my visible activity was moderate.
